narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 65% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic indicators like grades, test scores, and background factors, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which placed you at the lower end of your school's performance distribution that year; among students who passed the bar, the average was a much higher decile of about 5.91, so this stood out as a strong signal of potential challenges ahead. Next in influence, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 worked somewhat in your favor, as it pulled the prediction slightly toward passing—it's close to the 3.24 average for bar passers, showing solid prior academic effort. Your LSAT score of 28 also played a key role, nudging the outlook toward failure since it's notably below the 37.27 average among those who succeeded on the bar. Other aspects, like your upper-middle family income bracket—which aligns well with 45% of bar passers—and your plan to attend full-time, matching 93% of successful students, had less sway in this specific decision. Compared to typical admitted students, who tend to show consistently stronger deciles across years and higher entrance exam scores, your profile highlighted risks in later academic performance and testing that the model weighed heavily. While your Year 1 decile of 6 was right in line with passers' averages, it couldn't offset the later dip. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven flag based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and reflect on strategies to elevate your performance in advanced coursework, perhaps through tutoring or study groups. Strengthening these areas could shift future predictions in your favor, and many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting them directly. You're clearly capable, given your early grades and circumstances, so persistence here could open doors.
